title: Multi-Tenancy Architecture
content: This is the authoritative reference for NXGN Actions' multi-tenancy model. All
tenant isolation decisions and access patterns are documented here.
## Overview
Tenants are identified by a `TenantId` extracted from the JWT `tenant_id` claim.
The tenant boundary is enforced at both the application and database levels using
four complementary layers. All Actions endpoints run with tenant context from the
JWT — there are no pre-JWT endpoints.
The key difference from Identity: Actions applies RLS to **all** tenant-scoped
tables. There are no auth endpoints that need pre-tenant cross-org access, so
there is no reason to exempt tables from RLS.
## The Four Enforcement Layers
Each layer serves a distinct purpose. All four must be correctly configured for
any tenant-scoped feature.
### Layer 1: ITenantEntity Interface
Tenant-scoped entities implement `ITenantEntity`, which adds a `TenantId`
property. This property is used by the interceptors (Layer 3) and PostgreSQL RLS
policies (Layer 4) to enforce isolation.
Entities implementing `ITenantEntity`: all action domain entities, workflow
engine entities, compliance and escalation entities, entity translations,
tenant sequences, regulatory deadlines, and deadline types.
Entities that are **global** (no `TenantId`, no RLS): Language, Translation,
TranslationValue (platform-level, shared across all tenants).
### Layer 2: TenantEndpoint Base Class
All endpoints inherit from `TenantEndpoint` variants that extract tenant
context, actor identity, and language preference from JWT claims before the
handler runs. Missing or unparseable claims return 401 Unauthorized. No
business logic executes without a valid tenant context.
Unlike Identity, Actions has no endpoints that run without tenant context. Every
request arrives with a JWT containing a `tenant_id` claim — tenant selection
happens in the Identity platform before the user reaches Actions.
### Layer 3: Connection and Transaction Interceptors
EF Core interceptors inject PostgreSQL session variables into every SQL command,
bridging .NET request context to PostgreSQL RLS policies:
- **TenantConnectionInterceptor** sets the tenant context when a connection is
opened from the pool via `set_config('app.current_tenant', tenantId, false)`.
The `false` parameter makes this **connection-scoped** — it persists across
multiple transactions on the same connection and re-fires on every connection
acquisition from the pool (preventing tenant bleed between pooled connections).
- **TenantTransactionInterceptor** re-applies tenant context within transactions
via `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant` (transaction-scoped). This ensures rollback
reverts the tenant context within the transaction, and optionally sets platform
admin bypass.
Critical constraint: The connection interceptor uses `set_config(..., false)`
(connection-scoped), and the transaction interceptor uses `SET LOCAL`
(transaction-scoped, rollback-safe). Never use bare `SET` (session-scoped)
which leaks across pooled connections.
### Layer 4: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security
RLS policies on all tenant-scoped tables filter rows by the
`app.current_tenant` session variable. `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` ensures
policies apply even to the table owner role. If the session variable is not set,
no rows are visible — fail-closed by default.
## RLS Boundary
**18 tables WITH RLS** — all tenant-scoped entities: actions, action_evidence,
action_source_links, action_state_history, action_audit_events,
action_consultations, workflow_definitions, workflow_states,
workflow_transitions, compliance_profiles, escalation_rules, tenant_sequences,
regulatory_deadlines, deadline_types, and all four entity translation tables.
**8 tables WITHOUT RLS (global):**
| Table | Why No RLS |
| languages | Platform-level catalog synced from Identity; shared across all tenants |
| translations | UI string keys; global, managed by Platform Admin |
| translation_values | UI string values per language; global |
| tenant_configurations | FK anchor for all tenant-scoped tables; Id equals Organization.Id from Identity |
| users | Cross-tenant user records; Id = Identity sub claim; referenced by tenant-scoped entities via FK (Authentication module) |
| nav_sections | Platform-level navigation structure; seed-only, shared across all tenants (Action Portal module) |
| nav_items | Platform-level navigation items; seed-only, permission-filtered at query time (Action Portal module) |
| user_preferences | Per-user preferences (not per-tenant); keyed by UserId + Key (Action Portal module) |
An `RlsConformanceHealthCheck` validates at startup that all tenant-scoped
tables have their RLS policies. This prevents accidental policy omission after
migrations.
## Background Service Access
Background services (deadline alerts, escalation evaluation, overdue checks,
ledger verification) need cross-tenant access. These use a dedicated
`amps_background` database role with an unrestricted RLS policy, while the
application role remains strictly tenant-scoped.
## Platform Admin Bypass
Platform admins may need cross-tenant visibility for diagnostics and support.
The bypass mechanism sets `app.is_platform_admin` so RLS policies grant
cross-tenant access. This is used sparingly — unlike Identity, there are no
routine cross-tenant operations.
## Aspire Superuser Warning
The Aspire development environment connects to PostgreSQL as a superuser.
PostgreSQL superusers **bypass ALL RLS policies**, even with
`FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` enabled. This means:
- RLS bugs will NOT surface in local development
- Tenant isolation must be verified with non-superuser connections
- Integration tests should create a non-superuser role with appropriate grants
## Request Lifecycle
1. HTTP Request arrives with JWT Bearer token
2. FastEndpoints extracts JWT claims
3. TenantEndpoint extracts TenantId + ActorId + LanguageId
(returns 401 if claims are missing/invalid)
4. Endpoint handler executes, calls queries/commands
5. Query/command uses DbContext to execute SQL
6. TenantConnectionInterceptor fires:
Injects set_config('app.current_tenant', '<guid>', false)  [connection-scoped]
7. PostgreSQL evaluates RLS policy:
Only rows matching the tenant are visible/writable
